candidates.mechanism: A similarity-preserving router induces token-wise relational structure via orthogonality in its weights, which routes tokens to fast-weight experts whose hidden states are themselves models updated by self-supervised test-time training, so relational geometry regulates which expert undergoes test-time learning on each token.
candidates.transfer: SimBal's orthogonality-based, similarity-preserving routing loss transfers from MoE load balancing to govern expert selection in a test-time-training architecture where each expert's hidden state is a self-supervised learner.
candidates.open_problem: Does enforcing router orthogonality that preserves token similarity improve the stability and generalization of expert-wise test-time training compared to standard load-balancing losses?
candidates.primary_quote: a novel load balancing auxiliary loss that maintains token-wise relational structure by softly encouraging orthogonality in the router weights

candidates.niche_name: Rupture Dynamics of Test-Time-Training Hidden States
candidates.mechanism: Treating an LLM's hidden state as a self-supervised model that trains on the test sequence induces a 'rupture-like' adaptation front whose update speed and energy distribution are regulated by structural irregularities in the input, transforming off-distribution tokens into high-frequency representational radiation analogous to off-fault damage.
candidates.transfer: The off-fault-damage principle that local heterogeneity reduces propagation speed and boosts high-frequency radiation transfers to test-time-training dynamics, where input irregularities slow hidden-state convergence and amplify high-frequency update components.
candidates.open_problem: Does input heterogeneity in a test-time-trained hidden state measurably slow its update-propagation speed while increasing high-frequency components of its representational trajectory?
candidates.primary_quote: off-fault damage enhances high-frequency wave radiation above 1 Hz

candidates.niche_name: Damage-Regularized Orthogonal Routing for Stable MoE Load Balancing
candidates.mechanism: Off-fault structural damage induces high-frequency wave radiation and reduces rupture speed; transferring this principle, a SimBal-style auxiliary loss that softly encourages orthogonality in MoE router weights produces controlled 'damage' to the routing manifold that regulates token-routing rupture dynamics and routes load away from over-concentrated experts.
candidates.transfer: The orthogonality-inducing auxiliary loss mechanism from A transfers to B as a tunable perturbation that regulates the speed and energy distribution of a propagating routing/rupture front.
candidates.open_problem: Can an orthogonality-based auxiliary loss be calibrated to controllably damp high-frequency instability and slow over-fast convergence in MoE routing, analogous to how off-fault damage reduces rupture speed?
candidates.primary_quote: softly encouraging orthogonality in the router weights
